coredata.dc:title: Carbonized polymer dots‐silver nanoclusters nanocomposite with dual-emission for property ratiometric fluorescence and visual detection of temperature
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Menglei
Li, Pengjun
Wang, Mao
Liang, Yidan
Yang, Xudong
Pang, Shujie
coredata.dc:description: A dual-emission fluorescent nanocomposite was prepared. Carbonized polymer dots were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method using chitosan as the carbon source. Silver nanoclusters were synthesized by UV lamp irradiation using polymethacrylic acid as the stabilizer. The carbonized polymer dots and silver nanoclusters were self-assembled by electrostatic force to prepare a dual-emission fluorescent nanocomposite that responds to temperature changes. The carbonized polymer dots‐silver nanoclusters (CPDs-AgNCs) nanocomposites showed two emission peaks at 401 and 609 nm under the same excitation wavelength. It has an excellent linear relationship between 20 °C and 80 °C. And by introducing the dual-emission fluorescent nanocomposite into the membrane system, the temperature was successfully detected, which Broadens the application of rapid and convenient temperature detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots
Fluorescence
Nanoclusters
Temperature detection
Nanocomposite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the dual-emission sensor of CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites
Detection of temperature response of CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites
Preparation of composite films of CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites
Characteristics of the CPDs, AgNCs and CDs-AgNCs nanocomposite
Study of optical properties of CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites
Performance study of fluorescent polyvinyl alcohol films based on CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52273122 ), the Program of Jilin Provincial Department of Science & Technology (Grant No. 20220203060SF ), the Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, Jilin University ( sklssm2023030 ) and the Project of the Jilin Provincial Department of Education (Grant No. JJKH20220667KJ ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Transmission electron microscopy image of CPDs, scale bar: 10 nm (b) Particle size distribution of CPDs (c) Transmission electron microscopy image of AgNCs, scale bar: 10 nm (d) Particle size distribution of AgNCs (e) Transmission electron microscopy image of CPDs-AgNCs, scale bar: 10 nm (f) Particle size of CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites distribution.
(a) XPS full spectra of CPDs-AgNCs (b) XPS fine spectra of Ag 3d.

(a) UV–visible absorption, fluorescence optimal excitation and optimal emission fluorescence spectra of CPDs, insets are optical photographs of CPDs under daylight and UV lamp (b) Fluorescence spectra of CPDs under different excitations (c) UV–visible absorption, fluorescence optimal excitation and optimal emission fluorescence spectra of AgNCs, insets are optical photographs of AgNCs under daylight and UV lamp (d) Fluorescence spectra of AgNCs under different excitations.

Optimal excitation emission fluorescence spectra of CPDs, AgNCs and CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites, inset are optical photographs of CPDs, AgNCs and CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites in daylight and under UV lamp.
(a) Ratiometric fluorescence spectra of CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites from 20 °C to 85 °C (Ex = 334 nm) (b) CIE chromaticity diagram of CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites.

(a) Fluorescence spectral intensity of CPD-AgNCs nanocomposites from 20 °C to 85 °C (Ex = 334 nm) (b) Fluorescence intensity ratio (I401/I609) of CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites versus temperature response.
Optical photographs of CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites under daylight and UV lamp irradiation at 20 °C–80 °C.
Temperature-induced switching of the emission intensity ratio of CPDs-AgNCs dual-emission nanocomposites (alternating between 20 °C and 85 °C).
Visual images of CPDs-AgNCs@PVA fluorescent film composites for different temperatures in daylight and under UV light.
Schematic diagram of the preparation and response to temperature of CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites

head.ce:title: Carbonized polymer dots‐silver nanoclusters nanocomposite with dual-emission for property ratiometric fluorescence and visual detection of temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, Changchun 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, Changchun 130012, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dual-emission fluorescent nanocomposite was prepared. Carbonized polymer dots were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method using chitosan as the carbon source. Silver nanoclusters were synthesized by UV lamp irradiation using polymethacrylic acid as the stabilizer. The carbonized polymer dots and silver nanoclusters were self-assembled by electrostatic force to prepare a dual-emission fluorescent nanocomposite that responds to temperature changes. The carbonized polymer dots‐silver nanoclusters (CPDs-AgNCs) nanocomposites showed two emission peaks at 401 and 609 nm under the same excitation wavelength. It has an excellent linear relationship between 20 °C and 80 °C. And by introducing the dual-emission fluorescent nanocomposite into the membrane system, the temperature was successfully detected, which Broadens the application of rapid and convenient temperature detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Chitosan is a polycationic polymer that reacts readily with negatively charged compounds such as proteins, anionic polysaccharides, fatty acids, and phospholipids [1,2]. Some of the biological properties of chitosan, including antibacterial and antioxidant properties, are related to the polycationic nature of chitosan. In addition to its abundant, economical and renewable nature, chitosan has other benefits. For example, it possesses a large amount of nitrogen and various functional groups, including acetamide, amino and hydroxyl groups [3]. This is important because chitosan can be used as a single carbon and nitrogen precursor to quickly obtain N-doped carbon nanomaterials [4,5], which is advantageous for various applications. The outstanding properties of chitosan have been documented in different studies reporting the synthesis of chitosan-based materials, including porous carbon [6,7], nanoparticles [8], and, more recently Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [9], providing a broader perspective of chitosan-derived carbon materials.

Ag nanoclusters (AgNCs) are formed by stacking a finite number of atoms. Concerning this relatively small size, the band structure of AgNCs is discontinuous and decomposes into discrete energy levels. Thus, AgNCs have unique physical and chemical properties [10]. For example, quantifiable charging properties [11] and tunable fluorescence with high optical stability [12]. AgNCs are different from larger particle-size Ag nanoparticles. Typically, AgNCs are produced by reducing silver ions using reductants and capping agents. It tends to accumulate with each other and agglomerate to lessen the internal surface energy. In addition, the type of template and chemical structure, such as proteins, DNA and polymers, are decisive for the stability of the nanoclusters [13]. AgNCs are used for different applications such as biosensing [14], optical sensing [15,16], imaging [17], diagnosis of diseases [18], and antimicrobial applications [19], which are promising. Compared to the larger 10 nm silver nanoparticles, AgNCs have several advantages, including ultra-small size, large surface-to-volume ratio, luminescent optical properties, and extreme mobility.

Temperature is one of the most essential parameters for studying biological reactions in all living organisms. Accurate measurement of an organism's internal temperature and gradient can facilitate biological and biomedical research progress [20,21]. Many macroscopic and microscopic sensors have been reported for temperature sensing. Among the sensors used, fluorescence-based temperature sensors show great potential because they can serve as "non-contact" tools for simultaneous imaging and temperature sensing at the molecular level [22]. In recent decades, fluorescent polymers and metal nanoclusters (NCs) have been developed as typical fluorescence-based temperature detection sensors [23]. They have unstable optical properties under biological toxicity, environmental contamination and other conditions. These weaknesses significantly limit their further applications in biology and biomedicine.

The response mechanism of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterial thermometers to temperature has been a hot research topic. However, there still needs to be a unified explanation for the fluorescence mechanism of temperature sensing. This is because various carbon-based materials have been reported to control fluorescence, including fluorescent FNDs [24], P-dots [25], GQDs [26], and CDs [27]. As the temperature increases, the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of the most temperature-sensitive carbon-based materials changes to enable temperature detection. The temperature response mechanism of carbon-based fluorescent materials mainly includes changes in surface and molecular states, thermally activated non-radiative capture, spectral shifts, and broadening of the zero phonon line (ZPL) [28,29].

In this experiment, a fluorescent nanocomposite with dual-emission CPDs-AgNCs was reconstructed by electrostatic self-assembly of CPDs and AgNCs [30,31], and this CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposite had reproducible and stable temperature detection. A one-step hydrothermal method prepared the positively charged CPDs using chitosan as the carbon source. AgNCs were prepared using AgNO3 and polymethacrylic acid (PMAA) as the silver source and stabilizer. The AgNCs with PMAA as the stencil agent had a powerfully negative surface charge and could form CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites by self-assembly with the prepared CPDs [32]. The CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites showed two emission peaks at 401 and 609 nm under the same excitation wavelength. The blue fluorescence of CPDs gradually burst at 20 °C–80 °C and the red fluorescence of AgNCs was very stable. With the gradual increase in temperature, the fluorescence changes of the CPDs-AgNCs dual-emission nanocomposites gradually changed from pink to orange-red. On the other hand, the prepared nanocomposite PVA was compounded into a CPDs-AgNCs@PVA film, and the temperature was successfully detected. With its good controllability and reversibility, the synthesis process can increase the practical applications of dual-emission nanocomposites in different aspects of medical, environmental, and other temperature detection [33–35].

Chitosan (CS, deacetylation degree ≥95 %) was obtained from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Co., Ltd. Silver nitrate (AgNO3) was purchased from the Beijing Chemical Factory. Polymethacrylic acid (PMAA) solution (MW = 9400, 30 % w/w water) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (USA). All the reagents were analytically pure and could be used without further purification. Ultra-pure water obtained using a Milli-Q purification system (Merck, Germany) was used for all experiments.

Fluorescence spectra were measured by an LS55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) experiments were carried out using a JEOL-2000EX microscope. The UV‐vis absorption spectra were obtained using a UV-2450 (Tsujima Corporation, Japan)—Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra using an IS50 FT-IR spectrophotometer. Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS) experiments were carried out using a JSM-5610LV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with a VG ESCALAB MK II spectrometer (VG, Britain).

We first weighed 0.2 g of chitosan into 20 mL of 2 % (w/w) acetic acid solution, stirred to completely dissolve chitosan in the acetic acid solution, and then transferred the solution to a stainless steel autoclave and heated it for 7 h at 180 °C in a blast dryer. The resulting solution was centrifuged, filtered, dialyzed, and purified, and the resulting CPD was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

2.4 mL of polymethacrylic acid (PMAA) solution (0.1 mol/L) was added to 24 mL of silver nitrate solution (0.025 mol/L), after which the pH of the mixed solution was adjusted to 6 with nitric acid, mechanical stirring was performed, and the answer was irradiated with a UV lamp for 40 min under dark conditions. The colour of the solution changed from colourless to red, which could indicate the generation of AgNCs. The prepared AgNCs solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the dual-emission sensor of CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: 0.55 mL of CPDs was diluted to 6 mL, and 300 μL of the diluted solution was added to 3 mL of AgNCs solution and stirred for 30 min. Then dialysis was performed to remove the free individual CPDs and AgNCs. The prepared dual-emission CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites showed pink under UV light, and the fluorescence properties of the dual-emission nanocomposites were investigated. Two emission peaks, 401 and 609 nm, were observed at the same excitation wavelength for the CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites. The CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites were successfully prepared using the above method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of temperature response of CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity of the CPDs-AgNCs dual-emission nano system was recorded with a fluorescence spectrophotometer at different temperatures in the range of 20–85 °C for 5 min after stabilization to study its temperature responsiveness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of composite films of CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Weigh 5 g of polyvinyl alcohol into a three-neck flask, add 60 mL of distilled water, stir in a water bath at 80 °C for 20 minutes, cool to room temperature and then add the prepared nanocomposites, stir for 10 minutes, then pour the solution into the mould for natural air drying, dry to form CPDs-AgNCs@PVA composite fluorescent films, and finally remove the CPDs-AgNCs@PVA composite fluorescent films from the slides and store them in a refrigerator at 4 °C under refrigeration.

In the experimental section, we added the determination of fluorescence quantum yield. “Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CPDs and AgNCs was determined by a widely accepted relative method, where rhodamine 6G in anhydrous ethanol was chosen as a standard (QY ¼ 95 %). To minimize the reabsorption effects, the absorbance of the samples and rhodamine 6G was kept below 0.1. The integrated fluorescence intensity was obtained by calculating the area under the fluorescence curve. A linear fitting line was drawn using the integrated fluorescence intensity against the absorbance, with the intercept at zero. Finally, QY values were calculated using the following equation:
QY S
=
QY R
G S
G R
η S
η R
2
where QYS is the quantum yield of the sample and QYR is the quantum yield of the control (rhodamine 6G). GR and GS are the integrated intensity of the reference value and the slope of the absorption line of the sample, respectively. ηS is the refractive index of the standard sample, and ηR is the refractive index of the known sample.”

The preparation process of the dual-emitting CPDs-AgNCs fluorescent nanocomposites is shown in Scheme 1 . Firstly, CPDs with blue fluorescence emissions were prepared from chitosan. In contrast, AgNCs with red fluorescence emission were prepared from silver nitrate (AgNO3) and polymethacrylic acid (PMAA) as silver source and stabilizer, respectively. Then AgNCs and CPDs were stirred together by electrostatic self-assembly to prepare the dual-emission CPDs-AgNCs fluorescent nanocomposites [36,37]. The prepared CPDs-AgNCs dual-emission nanocomposites could be detected colourimetrically at temperatures ranging from 20 °C to 85 °C and showed high reproducibility. Based on this, the nanocomposites were successfully prepared as dual-emission mechanically stable CPDs-AgNCs@PVA composite films for proportional temperature sensing, which exhibited sound light transmission, flexibility, and elasticity. The obtained CPDs-AgNCs@PVA composite films with dual emission showed temperature response and reversible properties in the 20–80 °C range. Therefore, these materials have high application value in macroscopic dual-emission temperature detection and the further development of polymer composite modification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of the CPDs, AgNCs and CDs-AgNCs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Zeta potential tests were first performed on AgNCs, CPDs, and CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites, respectively, as shown in Fig. S1. It can be concluded that the surface of CPDs is positively charged with a charge of 2.9 eV. The character of AgNCs is negatively charged with an amount of 7.1 eV. The CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites that were made by mixing the two materials were still negatively charged and had a zeta potential of 5.7 eV. This means that CPDs and AgNCs can easily self-assemble into hybrid CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites through electrostatic interactions.

To further investigate the structural characteristics of the prepared CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites, we performed TEM test analysis of CPDs [38], AgNCs, and CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites, as shown in Fig. 1(a); we can see that the CPDs samples have a close to spherical structure and are uniformly distributed in aqueous solution with an average particle size of about 3.0 nm (Fig. 1(b)). In addition, the AgNCs also shows excellent dispersion and have a spherical structure in an aqueous solution with an average particle size of about 2.0 nm, The particle size distribution varies between 1.5 nm and 3.5 nm (Fig. 1(c), (d)). The assembled CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites could be seen by TEM, indicating that, indeed, a new monolith was self-assembled electrostatically with an average particle size of about 5.2 nm and a considerable variation in the particle size range between 3 nm and 8 nm (Fig. 1 (e), (f)).

The elements and contents of CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites were tested by EDS energy spectroscopy, and the results are shown in Fig. S2. The proportion of nitrogen and oxygen elements is high, and the contents (wt%) of each component of C, N, O, and Ag are 5.18 %, 22.66 %, 67.20 %, and 4.96 %, which proves the successful preparation of CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites.

As shown in Fig. S3, the functional groups of CPDs tested by FT-IR show firm peaks centered at 3453 cm−1, representing OH and NH stretching vibrations; peaks at 2989 cm−1, 1345 cm−1, and 834 cm−1 were attributed to CH bond stretching and bending vibrations; 1641 cm−1, 1561 cm−1, and 1439 cm−1 are attributed to CO, CC, and CN stretching vibrations, respectively; and the peak at 1132 cm−1 represents the characteristic peak of CO stretching vibration [39]. The FT-IR results demonstrate that the synthesized CPDs contain functional groups of oxygen and nitrogen, such as -OH, -NH2, and -COOH, on their surfaces and have excellent hydrophilicity. The FT-IR spectra of AgNCs are shown in Fig. S3 and reveal characteristic peaks of about 1647 cm−1, 1378 cm−1 and 1109 cm−1, which can be attributed to the CO, -CH3 and CC vibrations, respectively. In addition, a distinctive characteristic absorption peak of 1638 cm−1 appears in the spectra of AgNCs, indicating that the -COOH group is immobilized on the surface of AgNCs. Thus, these features suggest that PMAA-functional AgNCs were successfully obtained using in situ formed PMAA as a template. The vibrational peak of -CONH can be observed at 1641 cm−1, proving that CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites have been successfully synthesized by functional groups on the surface of AgNCs and CPDs [40,41].

XPS further analyzed the elements of the CPDs-AgNCs samples. As can be seen from the full spectrum (Fig. 2(a)), the absorption peaks at 604.0 eV,574.0 eV,532.0 eV, 400.0 eV, 283.1 eV, and 371.9 eV, corresponding to the binding energies of Ag 3p1/2, Ag 3p3/2, O1s, N1s, C1s, and Ag 3d, indicate that CPDs-AgNCs contain C, N, O, and Ag atoms with relative elemental contents of 74.2 % (C), 5.02 % (N), 14.05 % (O), and 6.74 % (Ag), respectively. It is shown that CPDs successfully bonded with AgNCs. A detailed analysis of the chemical bonding of the elements was performed. The C1s band of CPDs-AgNCs was divided into four peaks corresponding to sp2 carbon (C-C/C-C, 283.9 eV), sp3 carbon (C-O/C-N, 286.7 eV), carbonyl carbon (CO, 288.1 eV), and carboxylic acid carbon (COOH, 288.6 eV). The N1s band was decomposed into 399.9 and 401.3 eV for two peaks corresponding to the amino N and acetamide groups, respectively. The O1s band has two extremes at 531.7 and 533.2 eV for CO and CO, respectively. In addition, the oxidation state of Ag was also determined. From Fig. 2(b), the Ag 3d spectra of CPDs-AgNCs show that the binding energies of 368.5 eV (Ag 3d5/2) and 374.6 eV (Ag 3d3/2) are similar to those of silver atoms (368.0, 374.0 eV), indicating the complete reduction of silver ions and the dominance of the Ag (0) metal state in CPDs-AgNCs [40,42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of optical properties of CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Our CPDs-AgNCs fluorescent composites were compounded out of CPDs and AgNCs, respectively, and for CPDs, their optimal excitation wavelength was 326 nm. The optimal emission wavelength is 400 nm, and under the irradiation of a UV lamp, the CPDs' emitted blue fluorescence. At the same time, the light is yellow in daylight (Fig. 3(a)), and immediately after that, the CPDs were tested. The excitation dependence of the CPDs was tested, as shown in Fig. 3(b); when the excitation wavelength was gradually increased from 300 nm to 360 nm, the position of its emission peak did not change and did not have excitation dependence. In addition, the UV absorption spectra of CPDs were also tested, and the results showed that it has an absorption peak at 290 nm, which is due to the n-π leap. There is no substantial absorption peak for AgNCs before 460 nm. An apparent peak of AgNCs is found at 505 nm, which is attributed to tiny AgNCs' localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) [43]. The best wavelength for excitation is 511 nm, whereas the best wavelength for emission is 609 nm. AgNCs exhibit red fluorescence when exposed to a UV lamp. At the same time, the light was light red in daylight (Fig. 3(c)), and AgNCs showed apparent excitation dependence, as shown in Fig. 3(d), when the excitation wavelength changes from 460 nm to 550 nm. The position of the emission peak of AgNCs kept changing, and the fluorescence intensity is most vigorous at the wavelength of 610 nm, and the fluorescence quantum yields for CPDs and AgNCs were 3.8 % and 7.2 %, respectively. Showing an intense red fluorescence emission [44,45].

After mixing CPDs and AgNCs, the fluorescence spectra of CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites were tested. CPDs with blue fluorescence and AgNCs with red fluorescence formed CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites with pink fluorescence by self-assembly, and the best emission of CPDs and AgNCs when the excitation wavelengths were 326 nm and 511 nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4 , when the excitation wavelength was 334 nm, the CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites showed two emission peaks at 401 nm and 609 nm, respectively, which can further indicate that the CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites were successfully prepared. From the fluorescence emission spectra, the fluorescence intensity of CPDs with blue fluorescence emission did not change after mixing CPDs and AgNCs. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of AgNCs with red fluorescence emission decreased significantly, which can be considered a result of the absorption of part of the fluorescence of AgNCs by CPDs after self-assembly by electrostatic force. There may be fluorescence resonance energy transfer [46].

Their temperature-dependent responses were investigated to explore the potential applications of CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites. By recording the fluorescence at different temperatures using a spectrometer, the dual-emission nanomaterials were highly responsive to temperature.

From the fluorescence spectrum in Fig. 5(a), it can be seen that the emission peak of CPDs at 401 nm gradually decreases when the temperature increases from 20 °C to 85 °C. In comparison, the emission peak of AgNCs at 609 nm shows a slight decrease. In other words, as the temperature increases, the blue light of CPDs at 401 nm bursts, while the red glow of AgNCs at 609 nm remains stable. The self-calibration ability of CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites (fluorescence colour changes from pink to orange) may be due to the temperature-sensitive fluorescence mechanism of CPDs following Boltzmann distribution: with the increase of temperature, the molecular collision frequency and nonradiative leap rate increase with increasing temperature, while the radiative leap rate remains constant, and the temperature-dependent fluorescence properties arise from a coupling mechanism based on the temperature enhancement of nonradiative channels in the surface (trap/defect) state, where the fluorescence intensity of CPDs decreases significantly with increasing temperature, which is attributed to thermally activated nonradiative trapping, which is not thermally activated at low temperatures. Thus, excited electrons can radiate photons. However, once the temperature increases, more nonradiative channels will be thermally activated, and more excited electrons will return to the ground through nonradiative processes. As a result, the emission intensity of the excited state and the fluorescence intensity decrease. Meanwhile, a CIE chromaticity diagram was established to show the change in fluorescence colour of CPDs-AgNCs under the effect of temperature more distinctly, as shown in Fig. 5(b). The chromaticity diagram in the temperature range of 25–85 °C shows that the colour of this system is between red and blue [47], consistent with the fluorescence spectrum in Fig. 5(a).

As shown in Fig. 6(a), the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with the gradual temperature increase of 20–85 °C. There is a linear relationship between the two, as shown in Fig. 6(b), and the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I401/I609) and the temperature response between the two emission peaks was established. The linear regression equation of the fluorescence intensity ratio (I401/I609) versus temperature response (20 °C–85 °C) was calculated as y = 0.02128 x + 3.1248, R2 = 0.9987.

Observing the temperature change in the fluorescence colour, as shown in Fig. 7 [48], the intensity change of the two emission peaks resulted in a continuous fluorescence colour change. The fluorescence at 401 nm decreased with the increase in temperature. It was accompanied by a fluorescence colour shift from light pink to orange-red, discernible in the CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposite solution. This reflects that the prepared CPDs-AgNCs dual-emission nanocomposite can achieve both fluorescence and visual detection of temperature, and this dual-emission nanomaterial colourimetric detection is more straightforward to discriminate than monochromatic detection, which can further broaden the application scenarios of visible luminescence and biological and medical detection. [49]

Some reports attribute the mechanism to the thermal activation of non-radiative channels of surface (trap/ defect) states. The general picture is that the nonradiative channels were not activated at low temperatures, so the excited electrons could emit photons radiatively. On the contrary, as the temperature increases, more non-radiative channels became activated, and excited electrons got back to the ground state by nonradiative processes, leading to the decreasing fluorescence intensity. [50,51]

Stability and reproducibility are essential for CPDs-AgNCs dual-emission fluorescence nanothermometers. Therefore, the luminescence switching operation was continuously repeated in four heating and cooling cycles between 20 °C and 85 °C. As shown in Fig. 8 , continuous temperature cycling in the range of 20 °C–85 °C hardly affects the fluorescence intensity of the probes. Therefore, the prepared CPDs-AgNCs dual-emission fluorescent nanothermometer can be used as an ideal ratiometric temperature sensor with high reliability and accuracy, indicating excellent resistance to temperature variation. A simple electrostatic self-assembly method to prepare CPDs-AgNCs dual-emission fluorescent nanothermometer can be used as a PL thermometer and further used for applications. This novel nanothermometer shows many advantages regarding sensitivity, stability, accuracy, and reproducibility [50].

Because of the complexity of biological systems, the photoluminescence stability of CPDs-AgNCs must be considered before practical application. The strength of CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites in different concentrations of salt solutions was systematically investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. S4(a), which indicates that there is no significant change in the fluorescence intensity and still maintains good stability in high concentrations of salt solutions. Fig. S4(b) shows the fluorescence spectra of the prepared CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites measured by storing them in a refrigerator at 4 °C for 20 days, and it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity did not decrease significantly. Fig. S4(c) shows the fluorescence spectra of the prepared CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites that will be measured under different pH conditions. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity fluctuates significantly, and it remains relatively stable only under neutral conditions. Therefore, the prepared CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites can meet the required stability in practical applications. Thus, a simple electrostatic self-assembly method prepared a novel dual-emission fluorescent nanothermometer of CPDs-AgNCs. Compared with most previous reports, the technique used here retains the properties of CPDs and AgNCs and is also more convenient in operation. In addition, the obtained nanothermometer exhibits many advantages, such as sensitivity, stability, accuracy, and reproducibility, indicating its suitability for temperature detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance study of fluorescent polyvinyl alcohol films based on CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: An inexpensive, convenient, sensitive and efficient CPDs-AgNCs@PVA fluorescent film was developed to achieve the requirement of cost-effective and rapid determination of temperature response. Fig. 9(a) shows that the CPDs-AgNCs@PVA fluorescence film did not change significantly under daylight when exposed to different temperatures. In contrast, the film's colour gradually changed from pink to orange with the gradual enhancement of temperature under 365 nm UV irradiation. On the contrary, the film's colour gradually reverted from orange to pink fluorescence as the temperature gradually decreased under 365 nm UV irradiation (Fig. 9(b)). These results demonstrate that the prepared CPDs-AgNCs@PVA composite films exhibit good temperature response properties and can be used for macroscopic colourimetric visual inspection in a wide temperature range (20 °C–80 °C). The fluorescent films are low-cost, simple to operate, and highly sensitive and show great potential for use as fluorescent nanothermometers in both macroscopic temperature sensing applications and composite material development.

When a liquid is placed in contact with a solid substrate, the liquid front moves spontaneously towards equilibrium due to the out-of-balance Young's force. As the liquid front moves, the dynamic contact angle (θ
d ) and the contact-line position (x
CL ) relaxes towards an equilibrium state characterized by a static contact angle (θ
0 ). It is obvious that the contact line velocity (V) is a function of the driving force [52]:
V =
dx CL
dt
= f
cos
θ 0
– cos
θ d

The static contact angle of water was measured on the prepared CPDs-AgNCs@PVA fluorescent films by taking four points at different locations, as shown in Fig. S5 (a), which is the result of the PVA films tested without incorporating CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites, and the contact angle is in the range of 9.9°-18.3°, with good hydrophilicity, and the average contact angle is in the range of 15.1° The contact angle of CPDs-AgNCs@PVA fluorescent composite film was slightly larger after doping with CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites, as shown in Fig. S5(b) the contact angle was in the range of 22°-32.6°, the average contact angle was around 28°, but still <90°, with good hydrophilic performance.

A mechanical tester (100 N, AGS-X, SHIMADZU, Japan) was used during uniaxial elongation measurements. The length, width, and thickness of samples were set as 30 mm, 3 mm, and 3 mm, which were fixed with two tensile clamps. The speed was set as 100 mm min−1.

From Fig. S6, it can be seen that the breaking strength of PVA film is about 18.4 MPa, while the breaking strength of CPDs-AgNCs@PVA fluorescent composite film is about 46.7 MPa, which is mainly due to the fact that CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposites are embedded into the reticulation structure of PVA film, which will make the film's intrinsic structure stronger after compositing, so the CPDs- AgNCs@PVA fluorescent composite film has increased the fracture strength, so that the stability of the film is also increased.

Fluorescent nanocomposites with dual-emission CPDs-AgNCs were successfully combined by electrostatic self-assembly of positively charged CPDs and negatively charged AgNCs for nanofluorescent temperature sensors. The prepared dual-emission fluorescent nanocomposites have two fluorescence emission peaks at 401 and 609 nm. The blue fluorescence emission at 401 nm is responsive to temperature, while the red fluorescence at 609 nm is stable. The fluorescence colour of the CPDs-AgNCs nanocomposite gradually changes from pink to orange as the temperature gradually increases. It can be used as a promising nanothermometer with many advantages, such as sensitivity, stability, accuracy, and reproducibility. On the other hand, CPDs-AgNCs@PVA composite films have potential applications for colourimetric detection in an extensive temperature range (20 °C–80 °C). Therefore, these materials have high application value in macroscopic two-light temperature detection and the further development of polymer composite modification.
Menglei Sun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing -Original Draft, Writing- Review & Editing.
Pengjun Li: Data Curation, Visualization, Validation.
Mao Wang: Resources, Visualization, Investigation, Validation.
Yidan Liang: Data Curation, Formal analysis, Validation, Software.
Xudong Yang: Methodology, Review and Editing, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Project administration.
Shujie Pang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
No conflict of interest between all authors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52273122), the Program of Jilin Provincial Department of Science & Technology (Grant No. 20220203060SF), the Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, Jilin University (sklssm2023030) and the Project of the Jilin Provincial Department of Education (Grant No. JJKH20220667KJ).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2023.110559.
